features.description: A developer can configure, build, and run the standalone insight-io backend targets
steps: Run CMake configure from the repo root
Build the backend binaries and focused tests
Verify the expected binaries are produced
Run the focused test targets successfully

features.description: An operator can start the backend and verify the health endpoint
steps: Start the backend with a db path and frontend path
Call GET /api/health
Verify status is ok
Verify the backend version is returned

features.description: The device catalog lists canonical URIs that can later be bound to app routes
steps: Call GET /api/devices
Inspect a listed device and preset entry
Verify the device has a stable public name
Verify a canonical insightos:// URI can be formed from the listing

features.description: An operator can alias a discovered device and then use the aliased name in canonical URIs for direct sessions or app routes
steps: Inspect the catalog for a discovered device default name
Call the device alias endpoint
Verify GET /api/devices shows the aliased public name
Verify later session or app-source requests can use the aliased name in the URI

features.description: A user can create a direct logical session through POST /api/sessions without going through the app layer
steps: Call POST /api/sessions with a listed device name, preset, and delivery
Verify the session response is active
Verify the returned payload includes session and stream metadata
Verify the session can be inspected through GET /api/sessions/{id}

features.description: Two same-preset sessions can reuse shared capture while exposing separate delivery intents
steps: Create an RTSP session for a listed camera preset
Create an IPC-oriented session for the same camera preset
Inspect runtime status
Verify shared capture reuse is visible while each logical session remains distinct

features.description: A thin opener command can still create a direct session from one canonical URI
steps: Run the opener binary with one canonical URI
Observe the created session id
Inspect the created session through REST
Verify the same flow is representable through POST /api/sessions

features.description: An RTSP session exposes a consumable RTSP URL with the expected stream parameters
steps: Create an RTSP session for a video source
Read the rtsp_url from the session response
Probe the RTSP URL with an RTSP-capable client
Verify codec and frame dimensions match the promised delivery

features.description: An audio session can run alongside a video session and remain independently inspectable
steps: Create a direct audio session for a listed microphone source
Verify the session is active
Inspect the session through REST
Verify the video and audio sessions can be stopped independently

features.description: A stopped direct session can survive backend exit and be rehydrated explicitly after restart
steps: Create and stop a direct session
Stop the backend
Restart the backend with the same database
Inspect the persisted session record
Call POST /api/sessions/{id}/start
Verify a fresh runtime session is active

features.description: An idle app can connect without initial sources and expose an app id for later route binding
steps: Start an app without any initial source URI
Call GET /api/apps
Find the running app record
Verify the app id can be used for later target and source operations

features.description: A running idle app can declare multiple independent video routes after startup
steps: Create or locate an idle app
Create route yolov5 with kind video
Create route yolov8 with kind video
List app routes
Verify both routes are present and distinct

features.description: A running idle app can declare RGBD processing routes after startup
steps: Create or locate an idle app
Create route rgbd-view with kind rgbd
Create route orbbec-pointcloud with kind rgbd
List app routes
Verify both RGBD routes are present and distinct

features.description: A running idle app can receive a later video source injection bound to a declared route
steps: Start an idle app and declare route yolov5
Inject a listed camera URI with route yolov5
Verify the source record is created
Verify a logical session is linked
Verify the app receives route-scoped frame callbacks

features.description: A running idle app can receive a later RGBD source injection bound to a declared RGBD route
steps: Start an idle app and declare route rgbd-view
Inject a listed RGBD URI with route rgbd-view
Verify the source record is created
Verify bindings include color and depth roles
Verify the app receives route-scoped color and depth callbacks

features.description: One app can run a video route and an RGBD route at the same time with independent routing
steps: Create one app
Declare one video route and one RGBD route
Inject a camera URI into the video route
Inject an RGBD URI into the RGBD route
Verify both routed sources remain active independently

features.description: A simple app can still launch with one URI argument and immediately consume from it
steps: Build or run a simple app binary that expects one source
Pass one canonical URI as a CLI argument
Verify the app starts without separate route configuration
Verify the app receives the expected stream-role callbacks

features.description: A multi-route app can still launch from CLI by binding URIs to route names explicitly
steps: Build or run an app that declares multiple routes
Launch it with explicit route-to-URI CLI bindings
Verify each route receives the correct source
Verify no post-launch REST injection is required for that startup path

features.description: Top-level on_stream usage remains available through an implicit default route for compatibility and simple examples
steps: Write or run an app that uses top-level app.on_stream without an explicit route scope
Start the app with one compatible source
Verify the source binds to the implicit default route
Verify the existing Caps and Frame callback shape still works

features.description: App-source responses expose route metadata and resolved role bindings clearly enough for the SDK to attach
steps: Create an app and declare a route
Inject a compatible source
Inspect the source response
Verify route, route_kind, bindings, and session metadata are present

features.description: Stopping and restarting an app source preserves the durable app-source declaration while recreating runtime state
steps: Create an app, target, and source
Call the app-source stop endpoint
Verify the app-source row remains present and moves to stopped
Call the app-source start endpoint
Verify a fresh runtime session is linked to the same app-source record

features.description: Renaming a public stream alias does not rewrite an already running delivery or already attached app path in place
steps: Start a live delivery or routed app source
Rename the public stream alias
Inspect the running consumer behavior
Verify the old public attachment name stays in effect until the delivery is drained and recreated

features.description: After the old delivery is stopped, a fresh session reflects the new public stream alias
steps: Create a live delivery for a source
Rename the public stream alias
Stop the original delivery
Create a fresh delivery for the same source
Verify the fresh session exposes the renamed public stream name

features.description: The browser UI lets a user move from catalog inspection to app creation, route declaration, and source binding
steps: Open the browser UI
Inspect the listed canonical URIs
Create an app
Create one or more routes
Bind a listed URI to a selected route
Verify the routed source appears in the app detail view

features.description: The browser UI can show persisted app and source declarations after backend restart and let the user restart them explicitly
steps: Create an app, route, and source from the UI
Restart the backend with the same database
Reload the UI
Verify the persisted app and source declarations are still listed
Restart the source from the UI
Verify status updates reflect the fresh runtime session
